commentBody: Aslant

Interesting grid and interesting clues made for an interesting Saturday solve. A few feints and dekes, but a fair fight.
Thanks, Fritz.

I found this one challenging, I worked it steadily but slowly, but really bogged down in the NW corner.  POCOAPOCO was not a term I was familiar with, although thinking about Bolero (one long slowly built crescendo) led me to it.  After that I had enough to fill in the rest.

I thought the side by side by side downs at 31-33 made a nice troika: FACEPALMS, IMANIDIOT, NIGHTMARE.  Sounds like the ingredients of most of the dreams I remember.

Thanks Fritz.
I got stuck in the center because I was sure the 28 down was WADI. Legit, right?

Early stats from xwstats.com has this puzzle as Very Hard:

My personal experience corroborates this. I found it to be a worthy Saturday workout, although I will admit I solved it while watching the first US game of the World Baseball Classic, which divided my attention. 

Like Caitlin, I liked the crossing of MORON, IM AN IDIOT and AM I NUTS.
Very difficult. Not enjoyed.

Yay! A really good Saturday following a really good Friday. Let’s keep it going. I don’t care that today seemed to be a little easier for me than Friday. Keep them coming. I had to work really hard and focus just to bring it in at 30+ minutes with zero assistance.

The POCO A POCO crescendo of Bolero is the perfect musical image for this puzzle.

The snare drum pattern starts you off with an AWOL and an EMOJI.
Then the flute tune comes in to bring you ELBOW ROOM.
And it just keeps building and building until it crashes out and HOLY MOSES you are done!
Like so much else in today's NYT, I just wanted this puzzle to be over.
Punishing.
Even when I got the clue I still have no clue.
Overall quite a challenge. The IONE clue was tricky enough without also fitting ERIN

Doable and hard, but I didn't much like it for some reason.  A lot of it just felt forced to me somehow, and I didn't find anything very clever or fun.  Finished up pretty quickly and was glad to be done.

Looking back on the grid I like it less and less.

But at least it was the proper level for a Saturday.
Good, challenging puzzle, thanks Fritz. And your wife made some of my favorites last year - I remember loving all of her 2025 NYT puzzles (3 or more?)

Liked the puzzle, and I liked the workout (although I had OUTWITHIT rather than SPITITOUT for way too long).  

The only thing I wasn't a fan of was cluing DOTTED as "the central 13 black squares," because there were other black squares that were just as central or more central that aren't isolated, and it also feels odd to me to clue a square as a DOT.   It's certainly a legit way of cluing DOTTED, especially on a Saturday, but that issue combined with the not-the-only-central-squares issue seemed to me to be too much strain to include a clue that referred back to the puzzle.

Like others said, something about this puzzle just seemed off. Not that it was extremely hard, which it was, but that's fine for Saturday.

It was something else, like the answers to the clues were too vague or could've been a dozen other answers. And often the crosses were the same, or somewhere unknowable, so they were no help.

I couldn't even finish this one without Check Puzzle a few times, something I almost never have to do. Lost my gold star streak on this one.

My favorite Saturday puzzle in ages, made so by great clues and clever wordplay. 

I love the way NIGHTMARE and ELM ST are clued. 

Other favorites …
[One who makes a living from fish]  CARD SHARKS
[Takes off-road?} RECALLS
[What travellers typically have in America?]  ONE L
{Establishments where smoking is allowed]  RIB JOINTS

Well done, Fritz.
A good, hard Saturday puzzle. I liked all the off-center clues. It took me almost 20% longer than my Saturday average. More of these, please!
That has to be the single hardest NYT puzzle I’ve ever done. WOW.
Got ten minutes into this one and realized I was more annoyed than challenged, ended a decent streak to be done with it. Just didn’t care for it at all.

Nice one. Done and dusted. I'm just going to SPITITOUT:

Curious to know if anyone else feels that "it's not what you know" that fills the grids of late, it's how you are gently, and often cleverly led to a solution?

IMO, clues are made to enlighten, entertain, tease and torment. Sometimes chicken, sometimes feathers, but always an AHA! or a "Jeez, how'd they ever do that!?" and a few, "How in the @#!# did they get away with that?!"
However, I've finished each grid, including MIDI and MINI with no drama or dispair. Aside from a bit of flyspecking, the gold stars cometh. Are they getting easier?

Currently working through 1994-1995 NYT crossword archives. Maybe that has something to do with it. Either I'm finally getting the hang of it, or just dumb luck. 

Happy weekend solving, all.

Definitely a worthy Saturday.  I have to admit I was defeated and had to consult Word Play for help.  I put that down to the confluence of puzzle and solver and not the fault of either.  Once I got a few helpers it all came together and was quite satisfying.  No complaints from me.

Tough but fair. I got stuck in the NW. Probably spent almost as much time there as I did on the rest of the puzzle. 

I got ATONE AND AWOL right away but I had CNOTE instead of CSPOT. 

The only clue I’m skeptical of is 19A. I figured it out, but hesitated because, if we are discussing the word “travelers” (as opposed to actually travelers) then “have in America” should be “has in America.”
The streak is dead.  NW did me in, I needed a lookup to confirm KRONA and a Google to get CARDSHARK.  One step too obtuse a clue for my simple brain.  :-(

Tough but fair! I had “kanJI” filled for 21A, and “gIn JOINTS” for 13D for far too long. What were the odds that both fit, and fit with 14D’s I INSIST, but weren’t right?! Just goes to show that sometimes random consensus doesn’t guarantee correctness. 

When EMOJI finally came to me, I was pretty mad at myself.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Another endorphin-releasing, moderately disorienting instance of seemingly randomly filling two or three small gimmes on the first pass, embracing the enervating fact that this one will be the one to break my streak, pickaxing my way anyway through a corner off one of the initial little fills, painstakingly following a vein or two as bigger flakes fall with a couple of well placed taps which reveals a spot or two to place an explosive charge and after 45 minutes the entire lode is glitteringly revealed.
All of which makes me a bit richer and a little dizzy.

I wanted Holy Moly, but HOLY MOSES works as well. Some early lucky guesses made the grid not totally barren, but most of it was a wasteland.  The column jarred a few fills loose, but I was 4/5ths of the way to done when I had to do it, and that took away from my pleasure at the cockeyeed clues I had managed to unlock.  Are we being punished for all the grousing about "too easy" puzzles?  This one didn't make me happy.

Solid, solid workout. Tough but fair just like yesterday. Lots of terms/names I didn't know - POCO A POCO, LIANE Moriarty, ENHALO and so on but the crossings definitely helped. Also had no idea what a C-SPOT or a CARDSHARK was or even what a 'bone' is American slang for so had to do a couple of alphabet runs in the NW corner.

Count me as a big fan of this one, exactly the Saturday puzzle I want. I respect critics like B who disliked it for reasons other than difficulty, and I get it—this didn’t have all the flashy grid spanners and clever misdirects that you might enjoy—but to me that’s more of the Friday vibe, and for Saturdays I’m looking for more of a challenge and hopefully not just from arcane trivia or pedantic entries. Many of you just gave up saying it was too hard and that’s a shame. There was a ton I didn’t know here—fish as a slang for “mark”, ninnyhammer, WASH as clued (I had WADI forever), estoques, dancing on a WHIM…but somehow they all made sense at the end. For most of the rest it was challenging precisely because of the ambiguity and the rather sly somewhat sideways clueing.
And look, folks, we can’t have it both ways. You don’t want gimmes and more difficult puzzles? This is it. I didn’t see a lot of unusual proper nouns, obscure trivia, or questionable pop culture references everyone complains about here. I love clever punny misdirects as much as the next guy (and this had some—RIBJOINTS? STARPOWER?) but sometimes even those are more guessable. This was challenging mostly because of sheer tough but fair clues that had me stumbling, stabbing and intuiting all the way, and I think that’s terrific.
Last observation, REBUS didn’t sound Japanese but certainly looked like it might fit the bill, anyone else try that?
Good puzzle. Never heard of a stern test but sorted it out

Excellent puzzles both yesterday and today. Lots of interesting fill. The only long answer I got on my first try was SPIT IT OUT, most likely because I use that phrase more than I should 🤣 Thank you, Fritz and happy birthday to your wife! 🥳

Saturday sorted (NW was tricky for me) so now I can watch some FA Cup matches, yes! 

I liked OMNIVERSE (would adore a map of this), the apropos AM I NUTS, and HOLY MOSES. I have never heard anyone say HOLY MOSES ever, it’s hilarious.

Cheers!

Booyah, this was one STERN but highly satisfying Saturday crossword! Many times I thought I could hear the Grim Reaper of 27A coming after my streak — it was a near thing. My bane, almost, was my addiction to the 1960s Batman TV series: did anyone else have HOLYMOLEY for 69A? I was willing to rationalize away TOROL and DEETY for a very long time before I finally recognized the error of my ways.

My hat is off to Fritz for this splendid puzzle, and I am looking forward to his next Saturday Streak Smasher (can I trademark that??) sometime soon.

I have to wonder sometimes about crossword puzzle couples. I’m sure it’s mostly because people with similar skills and interests are drawn to each other, but sometimes I wonder if it’s that only another cruciverbalist can tolerate us. My wife isn’t one, and sometimes feel I’m on the edge of divorce when I’m ignoring her in a crossword solving reverie or, even worse, when I check out for days trying to create one in my head. I don’t know how she puts up with it, frankly.

I hope those who think that the Saturday puzzle has been much too easy of late are happy with this one. Granted, I’m a bit tired from skiing all day, but this took me as long as some of the really tricky puzzles from 20 years ago. 

Part of my trouble was being overly-Saturdayish early on, when I had WAdi instead of WASH. (I blame Frank Herbert for introducing me to wadi decades before I ever hiked in a wash.) Once I got rid of WAdi, I had much less trouble finding the answers. 

And a big thanks to ANYA Taylor-Joy, TONI Braxton and ex-PFC Wintergreen for their help. Without them, I wouldn’t have gotten anywhere with this one. 

Thanks for the challenge, Mr. Juhnke!

Now that was a Saturday!  I was becoming disillusioned with my personal best streak because so many puzzles of late have been, shall we say, undemanding.  But this puzzle demanded my full concentration and persistence.  I enjoy the sense of accomplishment when completing such a puzzle.

'The first thing you do if you sit at a new table is look for the fish. If you don't see the fish, you're the fish.' 

I read this years ago in an interview with a legendary card shark. This was his answer to the question, 'What's one thing you've learned in your career?' I later heard it in the movie Rounders. Not sure if the movie or interview came 1st or if that's just a standard of gambling folk wisdom.

Anyway, the clue 'one who makes a living off fish' ranks now as one of my all-time favorite clues. Even though I know that line, I still was barking up the wrong tree in the wrong forest, trying to make 'sushi chef' fit until I erased my whole top left corner and started again.

Liked the Catch 22 cameo, liked most clues, liked the grid... Well done!

I had a pun about ribs, but I'll spare you.

(Sorry for being short.)

That was one of the tougher ones lately. I made it harder on myself by having "out with it" confidently in place at 67A early on, which resulted in second guessing some ideas for crossing answers that turned out to be correct. Finally convinced myself to let it go and that freed up the solution for me.
A good puzzle but I won't call it a stern test. But then,  I wouldn't call anything a stern test.

I just gotta say, regarding 38A ["Does anyone else sense impending doom?"]...

We are in no way in any position to make this to be comparable to AM I NUTS.  AM I sane, maybe

I'm really being a bit serious here.  I haven't checked earlier comments but I can't be the first one to mention this.
Is no one going to talk about the swastika?

Boy, howdy, did that bucker almost slung me right off, to right under the bus.

I was soooo lost on 43A [You might dance on one.]  The only thing that seemed remotely possible was "prom", and the final "m" made that especially difficult to dismiss.  

When WHIM fell out of my synapses, I kvetched.  I kvetch every chance I get and I don't care who knows it.

And TWOS was also just nearly impossible for me.  I only got it afterwards.  At one point I had moOS, with a play on the word "low".  I feel off that limb HARD.

13D I had gInJOINTS, which made the NE a challenge.  I have no idea why I'm thinking about alcohol so much.

Really nice challenge.  I hope those who relish it found it here.

Got really tripped up by 13D. I already had the I from HELIO and was sitting in a cigarbar while solving. I was convinced that had to be right. Took a very long time for me to change it to RIBJOINT. Good tough solve
If we can have a word like estoques as a clue, why can’t urine be used in the fill?  I got 55D from the crosses then looked up estoques. I wish I hadn’t.

Ten minutes into the "solve", having entered almost nothing into the grid, I checked the puzzle and that's what I was left with: https://imgur.com/a/usepW5F I'm pretty certain that's my worst start to a puzzle at the NYT, ever. I'm not sure I will even attempt to continue. I think that makes this a truly proper Saturday challenge 🤣

April 17, 2010: IMBRUES clued as “Stained.”  Impenetrable to any discovery process except Autocheck, I believe. (I consider googling a legitimate part of the learning experience.) now THAT is a tough Saturday.  Today’s no romp, but doable

Difficult puzzles don’t bother me. A challenge is always good. And when it gets the best of me… maybe it’s just not on my wavelength, and that’s fair. 

But when there’s clue after clue that makes no sense, even when I reveal the answer… I don’t know what to do with that. Is it me? Is it the puzzle? Should it bother me this much? Should it bother me at all?

I guess there’s always Sunday.

Great puzzle, Fritz!  I almost gave up, but it was worth the struggle.  Loved AM I NUTS, a question I often as myself. Happy Birthday, Katie!  

Earlier post didn't pass the stern test of the moderators. Maybe censored for my comment about impending doom that contained the answer to 66A (as in “what did that ninnyhammer do now?”). Or maybe it was the FBI?😂
WASH and TWOS are still a mystery to me. And despite the explanation in the story, I still don’t understand RECALLS. Cultural differences no doubt.
Oblique simply for the sake of being oblique.
I don’t mind a tough solve with obscure hints to common words and phrases, but when the answers themselves barely qualify as words or phrases, I’m out.
That was a tough one, NW and SE corner took me over an hour.

V unhappy with 28D which clearly should have been WADI, and that it wasn't and was so poorly clued made the center impossible without resorting to help.

Agree with others' comments about 38A
I can't believe I could so strongly dislike a puzzle that brought up Pharaoh. What an absolute slog
I really wanted 13D to be a hookah bar.

This was mostly fun. The upper left corner gave me problems until it finally clicked.

The top two across clues gave me problems. After finally solving the puzzle, they strike me as similar in construction -- they're using words ('fish' and 'grab') in a euphemistic sort of way -- but only one clue (15A) has a question mark. It made me think one was literal and the other playful.

Add to it that I didn't know the units in MRIs or musical terms (although poco a poco makes sense even without knowing the musical term) and that corner was tedious.

Otherwise was a fun challenge.

Oh man sorry so many struggled and didn't enjoy but I loved this puzzle! It was sooo hard.  Took me a long time but I love it when I have to struggle to figure out every letter.

Luckily I forgot or didn't know WADI. My last error was KRONe and I couldn't figure out what LeND had to do with a plot. 

I think there's a tipping point between "this is impossible I hate it" and "this is impossible and I will solve it no matter what" and I stayed on the fun side of it.

This was definitely tough, but it's Saturday and it's supposed to be. Clues are going to be more vaguely worded and with misdirection, but once you see the answers they all make sense. After some of the recent gimme puzzles where peeps were blowing their personal bests away, it's nice to come back to a poser. This is the New York Times after all. It's supposed to be hard!
Hey at least we don't have anyone complaining today that it's too easy! Or 'felt like a Wednesday!'

[Cause of a cold sweat, perhaps] 

Seeeing my sparsely filled grid after the first pass.

This felt like a good old fashioned Saturday.  So much that I didn't know, forcing me to really work those crosses.  

WAdi is a word I most likely learned from a crossword puzzle eons ago.  I happily plugged that in, and held onto it until I had no choice.  

Thank you, Fritz.  I'd love to see you and Katie create a puzzle together.  She's one of my favorite constructors, and it would nice to see what devious trouble the two of you could cause.
NInnyhammer - Sounds like Will Ferrell in "Elf"?
That was a fun workout.
The dreaded CSPOT strikes again.

Shouldn’t it be, “What travellers typically HAS in America?” I got it because I’m a crossword veteran by now and recognize this kind of clue, but I think typically the editors still insist on puns working grammatically when read both ways, and this doesn’t. Probably should have been, “What a traveller typically has in America?” or just pick another British double L word.

This one did me in, streak ended after 201 days, but it was meant to be. Just some things in it I didn’t know and to play my own honest game with myself, would not have got and had to google. So Check Puzzle  turned on as I did the last clue, to dump the streak.  Back to one, to build my streak POCO-A-POCO. Begin again, Michael Finnegan…
Yet another puzzle with clunky clues and inane answers. NOPE for me today. Hopefully tomorrow is a better day. Have a good Saturday all. Going to the archives and the 1990s for something better.
27A, 45A and 33D capture the theme of this puzzle -- scary good.
The grid pattern was really great, but the left half of the puzzle was not really fun. It was just harder, but the wordplay did not reveal any interesting answers.

Fun Saturday!

The crossing of 1A and 9D cost me an extra ~5 minutes at the end (including a double flyspecking pass), followed by a grinning 32D — and I’d like to think I walked right into a trap that Fritz/Shortz deliberately laid.

That is: when I finally “got” 1A (delightfully clued, btw), I had it as CARDSHARp; and simultaneously, I was ready for 9D to be just about *anything*, on account of the nod to Icelandic. And finally, I certainly wasn’t confident about POCOAPOCO in its totality, even as the final letter was highly constrained by the crosses.

If 9D had used any number of other European countries as the modifier (e.g. “Swedish”, “Danish”, or “Czech”), I probably would have had the “K” before half the other letters in CARDSHARK. Maybe it’s just me, but Icelandic added such a nice wrinkle to the plot.

Touché, and thank you :)
I thought it was a fair Saturday. Needed help but in the end the music came on. Not sure why all the complaints.

To create a puzzle as challenging as this one, I believe it takes complex thinking and a deft sense of connection between hints of meaning in words and phrases. I am grateful to the constructor and the editing team for their time and efforts.
This is a puzzle, certainly.
I saw the pattern of the center as a "tilted square", so I had TILTED instead of DOTTED for the longest time. FACEPALM!
As we know from Friends, "it's a CARDSHARp, not a CARDSHARK". Frustrating one today.

Nearly identical to yesterday's for me---solved without assistance in just over 20 minutes. A very fine Saturday construction. Some very cute clues, like "Takes off-road?" for RECALLS.

Would love to see more themeless puzzles from Mr. Juhnke!

Oh dear, we finally get given a proper Saturday challenge and the comments section is predictably flooded with people complaining that the puzzle was too difficult.

I didn't think there was anything unfair in this and my problems were largely of my own making (sticking rigidly to ON tempo rather than IN tempo).

After a mighty struggle with this one, I was relieved to see that the comments aren’t filled with the currently popular chorus of “ridiculously easy”, “shoulda been a Tuesday”, “puzzles were harder in my youth”, etc.

Excellent Saturday, Fritz! Suitably annoying and thoroughly satisfying. Love the idea of a Fritz & Katie puzzle. Would it be like mixing oil and water or more like slipping vermouth into a glass of gin?

Y’all know I’m always grateful to the constructors and editors for sharing their talent and efforts.  Alas, my mileage varied today.  A lot.  I found most of the clues to be too esoteric, obscure, or misleading for me, even for a Saturday.  I could never get a toehold in the grid, and my eventual reaction to many of the answers was “whaaa….?”

In short, that’s a 25D from me today.

Y’all have a peaceful day and be kind to yourselves and one another.

Two days in a row now that I get the pop up telling me I have completed the Midi, but I do not get the star. Anyone else having this issue?

Killer regular crossword though. Hurts so good.
This was the least fun Saturday I can remember. Seems to be designed for a very specific kind of solver.

When the puzzle's giving you trouble, there's wisdom the the advice to walk away from it. 

On the first pass I had virtually nothing filled in, and thought for sure that this puzzle would be the one to break me. So I left it and went back to my computer to research the Upright MRI I'm scheduled to have late afternoon. I wanted to see a picture of the unit for maybe the 10th time. (I hate these tests). And there was a picture of a machine called the TESLA.

Then I went back to the puzzle, and there was the clue that I hadn't seen before, and wouldn't have known. But now I did! Walking away from the puzzle works even when the connection isn't as obvious.

Those two dollar bills are terrific. When I had to clean out my parents' house, I found an envelope filled with them that my dad had collected. I subsequently used them as tips when traveling, and they were greatly appreciated, especially when I would tell the recipients that they were genuine legal tender. Many said that they'd never spend them.

Finally, a challenge worthy of a Saturday.
Like raising children, this puzzle gets more fun the more it recedes into the past.
That’ll do, Fritz.
Great puzzle!
Hoooweeee, THAT was a Saturday that WAS a Saturday! Practically scratched myself bald on some of the clues. This here puzzle is a work of art and an example to aspire to. Terrific job Fritz!

Um...

Yes?

Yes.

This.

FACE PALMS.

HOLEY MOSES, that was a DROLL NIGHTMARE of a puzzle.

FINALly, some Saturday-level cluing.
Thanks, Fritz.

OK, here I go posting again, when I usually lurk. I am not nearly as adept as the cruciverbalists on this chat, but I am enjoying (and solving!) the difficult Fridays and Saturdays of late. Admittedly, I think my streak of 74 is due to so many of the prior Fridays and Saturdays of the last months being so easy.

But this week's Fri and Sat and last week's have made the solving so challenging and wonderful. A prior poster, @SP put it best: "I didn’t see a lot of unusual proper nouns, obscure trivia, or questionable pop culture references everyone complains about here." No Cheats, as I call them. And so fun!

I did have SUSHI CHEF for 1A as another solver did. That threw me. Then I had ETHNO for -centric (16A) for the longest time until I deleted almost everything in that block and found HELIO. And someone else mentioned that for the first 10 minutes, nothing was filled in. Me too! I did say to myself, "You had a good run, so if this is the one that blows your streak, at least it's a deservedly challenging, well-edited one."

So thank you, Fritz (and Will!), for reverting back to brain-stretching status in creating worthy Friday and Saturday puzzles. I know from these posts that I'm in good company.
Whew, tough one!  Took me 14 minutes over my average.
VERY difficult, had to do a lot of guessing, but I got it with no look-ups in 1hour and 22 minutes.
Wow, one of the toughest in recent memory! I thought the NW corner would be my streak-breaker. And it didn't help my SE efforts that I was sure it was ERIN hiding in "electioneERINg"!
Can someone explain the reasoning behind 53 across?
Wow - that was tough.   The only thing that seemed unfair was that I think the clue for 1a should have had a question mark.

What a fun Saturday! When David Sedaris said, in one of his great essays, that "Friday and Saturday NYT crosswords require the ability to bend spoons with your mind" this was the kind he was talking about!

So much fun to get a “two cups of coffee” puzzle on a Saturday.  A beautiful, startling, and somewhat imposing grid, scads of clever clues and misdirects, and not an Oreo in sight. Like a previous commenter, I think the clue for 19A was ungrammatical, but even that added a little frisson to the morning’s solve. Bravo Mr. Juhnke!
what does an MMA fighter do?

One of my favorite puzzles ever. Difficult but solvable. The foreign clues at least gave a hint to their subject matter. When I say foreign, I mean completely unknown to me. 

My first pass resulted in very few filled squares, some with dubious confidence.

Long answers were very difficult to guess without crosses. Luckily a few came to mind with only one. The good news is that once solved, confidence is high and a lot of information gained. 

Thank you Fritz.

Yikes!

Tough one, definitely had me wondering about my streak, but I was just thinking yesterday that part of the key is to just remember that it's not *if* you're going to solve it, but *when*.

Probably only two or three filled-in answers on my first Across pass, and I don't think any of them survived but you have to start somewhere. Poly before OMNI, ethnO before HELIO, Cnote before CSPOT, and on and on.

66A brought back fond memories of Lord of the Rings, with Samwise quoting his Gaffer. "Yer nowt but a ninnyhammer, Sam!" he would say to me, that being one of his words.

A worthy challenge for a Saturday.
Couldn’t understand many of the answers - clues seem overly cryptic to the point where I almost didn’t care anymore if I lost my streak

I am thoroughly enjoying these difficult Saturday puzzles.

I am disappointed, but not surprised, to see many here whining that it was too difficult. People, Saturdays are supposed to be difficult. This wasn't even the hardest Saturday I've done. If you don't want a challenge, why are you doing the NYT crossword puzzle in the first place? HOLY MOSES.

Phewwweeee.  That was tough. 20ish minutes more than or roughly double my Saturday average. Had to put it down last night and sleep on it, which I usually don't have to do.  Almost a streak breaker.  

I had CNOTE in at 1D for a long time, and stared at an almost blank NE for 30 minutes before it started to fall.  I agree that some of the fill didn't match the clues very well, like AMINUTS or STERN for a hard test.  But they were gettable with crosses.  

For some reason I had SKOSH in for the fluid clue for a long time, and that held me up.  I had never heard of an ANIL. Learn something new every day.
Can anyone explain 8D TAKES OFF-ROAD.  How does this equate to RECALLS?

Ok for what it's worth I thought this was the best Saturday puzzle in a while . Yes some of the answers were obtuse - but fair once you got there .I'll admit even with the explanation I don't understand RECALL for Take it off-road , but other than that it all made sense to me . Not knowing things like the lingo for a CARD SHARK is part of the fun- ie learning something new . If it was straightforward and easy it wouldn't be a real NY Times Saturday . Check out the archives if you don't believe me. 
Years ago when I started doing the Times puzzle daily I couldn't do a Saturday at all . Now I finished this one in about 22 minutes without look ups . It's just a matter of doing enough tricky puzzles to get the hang of them and ultimately enjoy them .
Good job Fritz and Happy Birthday Kate .
I did not like this one
One of the most challenging puzzles in a long time.

Wow. I concur with many of you. This was an excellent Saturday puzzle, but it was a tough one! And it certainly didn’t help that I stuck with “out with it!” at 67A for way too long. BTW- The “Ts” in “out with it” and SPIT IT OUT align perfectly- so some of my down answers were working just fine. Cue face palms and Dohs! Some of the best clues I’ve seen in a long, long time.

[I can be this]

Two very challenging puzzles in a row for me. Nice.

To echo ninnyhammer, 9D could have been clued as money in Lillehammer, which is KRONe and would necessitate changing LAND to LeND.





DOTTED

What a great Saturday puzzle! I really struggled with it, especially
the SW.

Nice and tough. Enjoyed it.

I had Have Mercy sitting at 69A for awhile before sorting things out, so now I have La Grange by ZZ Top stuck in my head. No complaints.

Really hard!
A few clues have already been explained, but I need CSPOT - can't work out what the clue or the solution means. Thank you.

Add my name to the list of people who were flummoxed in the middle by WAdi.  
When I first saw the clue "Dry streambed", I thought to myself, "Oh, I know that. What is that?"  And then, when I got the WA__, thought, "Yes!  WAdi!  I knew I knew that!"

I adore Ravel's Bolero. Whenever I've heard it, I've always thought of the film "Les Uns et Les Autres" by Claude Lelouche (known as "Bolero" in the U.S.)

Until now. 

From this moment on, whenever I hear it, I will think of this puzzle and its slow-burn crescendo, POCO A POCO reminding me, to the very end, of how still unfit I am to gold star a NYT Saturday puzzle that measures up to its own famous gold standard. 

Yup, I'M AN 1D10T sitting right next to FACE PALMS....you know me so well, Mr. Junhke! 

The only progress I've made in five years is that it no longer takes me 3 hours  – that's with auto-check! – but a mere one hour and 20 minutes – and that's using "check puzzle" more than a few times and having to sleep for 8 hours between start and finish.

That said, I welcome being put in my place as a relative newbie (never having solved any xword before the NYT sucked me in.)
I welcome it because it makes me happy for the long-time solvers who I believe deserve puzzles more up to the gold standard they expect. 

They are, after all, the ones who showed me the ropes when I lurked in the comments, taking in each of their explanations and tips, adding immeasurably to the NYT articles I'd read to learn about xword lingo and cluing.

To this day, they continue to tirelessly take the time to answer questions and teach those who are interested enough, patient enough, and perhaps humble enough, to "look, listen, learn" without expecting mastery from the get-go. (Continues...)

From this moment on, whenever I hear it, I will think of this puzzle and its slow-burn crescendo, POCO A POCO reminding me, to the very end, of how still unfit I am to gold star a NYT Saturday puzzle that measures up to its own famous gold standard. 

Yup, I'M AN IDIOT sitting right next to FACE PALMS....you know me so well, Mr. Junhke! 

The only progress I've made in five years is that it no longer takes me 3 hours  – that's with auto-check! – but a mere one hour and 20 minutes – and that's using "check puzzle" more than a few times and having to sleep for 8 hours between start and finish.

That said, I welcome being put in my place as a relative newbie (never having solved any xword before the NYT sucked me in.)
I welcome it because it makes me happy for the long-time solvers who I believe deserve puzzles more up to the gold standard they expect. 

They are, after all, the ones who showed me the ropes when I lurked in the comments, taking in each of their explanations and tips, adding immeasurably to the NYT articles I'd read to learn about xword lingo and cluing.

Here’s a little challenge for anyone who likes both crossword answers that push the limits on rarity, and Google Books Ngram Viewer. In the NYTXW of Feb. 24, 1995, the clue for 38D is [Irate] and the answer is ASEETHE. That’s right folks: aseethe. That ire-itating (enirking?) little word has appeared only the one time in this hallowed puzzle’s history, I believe. The challenge is to find another (non-proper noun) word used in the NYT crossword that for Ngram data between 1900-2000 actually has a pulse, but is lower than ASEETHE. So far, UFFDA possibly qualifies, but it really has zero appearances until about 1980. Enhalo, as seen today, is a bit more common (see results at link below). https://tinyurl.com/mvwe3cny
